coredata.dc:title: Online state of health estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on a dual self-attention multivariate time series prediction network
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huanyu
Li, Jun
Liu, Xiaoxi
Rao, Jun
Fan, Yuqian
Tan, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: With the development of cloud and edge computing, deep learning based on big data has been widely utilized for lithium-ion battery state of health (SoH) online estimation, where improving the accuracy, robustness, and real-time applicability are current research challenges. Focusing on these points, this paper proposes a novel health feature analysis and screening method and a dual self-attention multivariate time series estimation network (DSMTNet). First, the correlation between all feature sequences and the SoH is evaluated by the Pearson correlation coefficient method. On this basis, the 15 most relevant features are selected by the light gradient boosting machine method as the DSMTNet input. Next, multi-head convolutional neural networks are utilized for encoding the battery features to enhance the final representation learning results. Then, a global attention unit is utilized to model the weights of the encoded feature sequences to extract common information, and a local attention unit is chosen to obtain the differentiated information, which is used as supplementary information. Finally, the accuracy, robustness, and computing time of the DSMTNet method are verified on experimental data. The results prove the superiority of the proposed method compared with other implemented approaches.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State of health
Feature screening
Dual attention mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Proposed hybrid neural network model for SoH estimation
Analysis of the estimation results of the different models

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of an intelligent cloud battery management system (the SoH part) based on an intelligent connected vehicle management platform.
(a) Voltage and current response for the cycling profile; (b) voltage curves of battery B05 during the aging cycles; and (c) SoH data of the tested batteries.

The relation and importance of features. (a) The area surrounded by the charging voltage curve; (b) Pearson correlation coefficient levels for different time intervals and regional interval features; (c) time intervals and the regional interval feature sequence importance ranking.
Illustration of splitting the source dataset into training data and prevalidation data for pretraining.
Schematic of the proposed DSMTNet approach: (a) the structure of the model; (b) schematic of attention units.

Capacity estimation results of different features. (a) Capacity estimation results and errors of B04; (b) capacity estimation results and errors of B05; (c) capacity estimation results and errors of B06; (d) RMSEs of the capacity estimation results of removed features, all features, and selected features; (e) MaxEs of the capacity estimation results of all features, selected features and removed features.

SoH estimation results of different machine learning methods. (a) SoH estimation results and errors of the four machine learning methods. (b) SoH estimation results and errors of the four deep learning methods.

RMSEs and MAXE of the segment estimation results for B04 and B05. (a) RMSEs and MAXE of the capacity estimation results for B04 at different segments; (b) RMSEs and MAXE of the capacity estimation results for B05 at different segments; (c) RMSEs and MAXE of the capacity estimation results for B05 at different start positions; (d) RMSEs and MAXE of the capacity estimation results for B05 at different start positions.

row.entry:
(a) Charge the battery with a CC mode of 0.5 C until the voltage reaches 4.2 V, then charge the battery with a CV mode until the current is reduced to 0.1 C.
(b) Keep the battery at rest for 120 min.
(c) Discharge the battery to cutoff voltage (2.5 V) at 0.5 C.
(d) Keep the battery at rest for 120 min.
Repeat the pretreatment steps five times.

(a) Charge the battery with a CC mode of 1 C to 4.2 V, then a CV mode to 0.02 C.
(b) Keep the battery at rest for 30 min.
(c) Discharge the battery to cutoff voltage (2.5 V) at 3 C.
(d) Keep the battery at rest for 60 min.
Repeat the aging cycle steps fifty times.

(a) Charge the battery with a CC mode of 1 C to 4.2 V, then a CV mode to 0.02 C.
(b) Keep the battery at rest for 30 min.
(c) Discharge the battery to cutoff voltage (2.5 V) at 1 C.
(d) Keep the battery at rest for 10 min.
(e) Discharge the battery to cutoff voltage (2.5 V) at 0.05 C.
(f) Keep the battery at rest for 5 min.
Repeat the aging cycle and capacity calibrating step twenty times (approximately 1000 cycles).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The typical conditions and parameters of charging data.
MAEs, RMSEs, MaxEs and MAPEs of SoH estimation method results with feature selection.
The schematics of FNN, BiLSTM and 1DCNN.
MAEs, RMSEs, MaxEs and MAPEs of the SoH estimation results.
Number of parameters and predicted computing times.
Positions of 3 different segmented data samples.
Positions of 5 different segmented data samples.

head.ce:title: Online state of health estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on a dual self-attention multivariate time series prediction network

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, Guangdong Province, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, Guangdong Province, China, 510006

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the development of cloud and edge computing, deep learning based on big data has been widely utilized for lithium-ion battery state of health (SoH) online estimation, where improving the accuracy, robustness, and real-time applicability are current research challenges. Focusing on these points, this paper proposes a novel health feature analysis and screening method and a dual self-attention multivariate time series estimation network (DSMTNet). First, the correlation between all feature sequences and the SoH is evaluated by the Pearson correlation coefficient method. On this basis, the 15 most relevant features are selected by the light gradient boosting machine method as the DSMTNet input. Next, multi-head convolutional neural networks are utilized for encoding the battery features to enhance the final representation learning results. Then, a global attention unit is utilized to model the weights of the encoded feature sequences to extract common information, and a local attention unit is chosen to obtain the differentiated information, which is used as supplementary information. Finally, the accuracy, robustness, and computing time of the DSMTNet method are verified on experimental data. The results prove the superiority of the proposed method compared with other implemented approaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have been used as energy storage devices in many renewable energy systems due to their advantages of high energy density, good stability and long service life (Tan et al., 2021a). However, the performance of LIBs degrades during continuous charging/discharging, resulting in capacity fade or power attenuation (Fan et al., 2019). Therefore, health diagnostics are crucial for both manufacturers and customers of electric vehicles (EVs) to ensure the safety and reliability of LIBs (Fan et al., 2020). The state of health (SoH) is one of the critical parameters of battery systems, and its variation directly affects the performance, reliability and safety of battery packs (Pan et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). However, the complex internal electrochemical configuration and uncertain external operating environments make the degradation process of LIBs extremely complicated, and the degradation process varies even among otherwise identical batteries, which limits the accuracy of the SoH estimation of LIBs (Shu et al., 2020). Therefore, the fast and accurate SoH estimation of LIBs has been an important research topic. In this paper, SoH is defined from the perspective of capacity, and SoH is defined as the ratio of actual battery capacity to its initial value. When SoH decays below 80%, a battery is considered to have reached its maximum service life and needs to be replaced (Guo et al., 2019).

Various methods for SoH estimation have been widely proposed. Common model-based methods design an equivalent circuit model to simulate the internal working mechanism of a battery and then use various optimization algorithms and observers (Tan et al., 2021b), such as the Kalman filter (Tian et al., 2021; Couto et al., 2019), particle filter (Lyu et al., 2021), and sliding mode filter (Ning et al., 2018), to identify the model parameters and SoH values. One of the common methods is the use of electrochemical models that are closely related to aging, which apply partial differential equations to model the mass and charge transfer kinetics (Ashwin et al., 2017). This type of approach can solve the problem of simultaneous online parameter and state estimations, but it depends on the modeling accuracy, and the accuracy, stability and convergence of the observer are strongly influenced by the initial values.

In contrast to model-based approaches, a kernel-based approach provides a different tactic in battery SoH estimation to prevent discrepancies in the raw data. In this approach, kernel-based learning algorithms, such as support vector machines (SVMs) (Feng et al., 2019; Khumprom and Yodo, 2019), correlation vector machines (Pan et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2021), and Gaussian process regression (Tagade et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2021a), are presented to estimate the battery capacity and usually use eigenvalues extracted from the experimental results. Deng et al. (2019) proposed training a support vector regression (SVR) model that uses four features, which include the charging process constant current (CC) charging time and capacity and the constant voltage (CV) charging time and capacity, to predict SoH, and the accuracy of the algorithm was verified on the NASA battery aging dataset. Zhang and Guo (2015) demonstrated that by incorporating an accelerated particle swarm optimization algorithm into the multikernel relevance vector machine method, the accuracy and robustness of the battery capacity estimation are obviously improved. Nevertheless, the SVM method suffers from a lack of uncertainty management and sparsity. More importantly, the number of support vectors is highly dependent on the error boundary.

Recently, data-driven approaches based on deep learning have received increasing attention in academia and industry due to their flexibility and model-free advantages (Li et al., 2019). Such approaches establish a nonlinear mapping between measurable electrical quantities (or electrical characteristic quantities) and SoH values through machine learning techniques in the offline phase and then obtain the SoH by using the newly observed electrical quantities and the trained machine learning model in the online phase. These methods prevent modeling and parameter identification problems in model-based methods and represent practical methods for SoH estimation. Lin et al. (2013) proposed a probabilistic neural network (PNN) approach to predict SoH as well as remaining life information and achieved encouraging results. Dai et al. (2019) trained a 3-layer forward propagation neural network and combined it with Markov chains to simulate the long time response of a battery aging process for SoH estimation. Shen et al. (2020) presented a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) model based on large volumes of charge data in the online estimation of LIB capacity. A recurrent neural network (RNN) was introduced by Eddahech et al. (2012) for the SoH estimation of LIBs, which combines an RNN with a model of the equivalent circuit to predict the degradation of battery performance. Chemali et al. (2018) proposed a long short-term memory (LSTM)-based method for SoH estimation to overcome the vanishing gradient problem caused by useless RNN information. The studies conducted with these methods demonstrate that any complex nature of dynamics and non-linearity can be modeled effectively. Another advantage of data-driven models is that they can deal with the time-varying internal parameters of the model by receiving new data and re-training (Hasan et al., 2020).

However, the data-driven method faced some issues that still should be noted: (i) In practice, due to improper manufacturing processes, there is poor consistency among LIBs, which leads to a different SoH profile for each battery. All the above studies ignore the impact of this issue on the model estimation accuracy. (ii) The raw curve contains too much noise to be directly utilized for SoH estimation. This fact has motivated many scholars to employ various filtering techniques for input curve smoothing (Li et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020). However, no quantitative analysis has achieved an optimal balance between curve smoothness and feature retention thus far. (iii) In addition, almost all of the abovementioned approaches are selective about the raw data. However, in many practical situations, the batteries in vehicles are most likely to be charged starting from its SOC laid between 20% and 80% and ending with an SOC between 70% and 100% (Fan et al., 2019). Thus, the raw data of the complete period are difficult to obtain due to the uncertainty in user behavior on charging and discharging schedules. To deal with this problem, it is necessary to develop a new battery capacity estimation method that is robust for segment data whenever acquired from complete period input data.

To address the above problems, a dual self-attention multivariate time series estimation network (DSMTNet) is proposed for the accurate and robust SoH estimation of LIBs. The main advantages of the proposed method are summarized as follows: (i) The model can cater to diverse charge data series of varying lengths by using 1-D convolutional filters of multiple lengths to capture features at different time scales. (ii) The proposed architecture allows ample representation learning in addition to the temporal relationships within charge series data. (iii) An encoder with global and local self-attention modules is applied to further emphasize the correlation between different battery sequences. It helps capture dependencies that 1-D convolutional filters cannot capture.
The proposed feature selection and deep learning method involves three main steps, summarized as follows:

1 First, features that can describe battery aging from the battery charging curves of real data measured in a laboratory are selected, and the light gradient boosting machine (lightGBM) method is utilized to fit the importance of all features relative to SoH.
2 Second, three different scales and depths of a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1DCNN) are utilized to capture complex mixtures of global and local temporal patterns.

3 Third, global and local self-attention modules are combined with multi-heads 1DCNN to learn the dependencies between different battery sequences, which include the common characteristics of features and the individual characteristics of batteries.

Based on the intelligent connected vehicle management platform (ICVMP), as shown in Fig. 1, an intelligent cloud battery management system (BMS) is developed to realize battery state real-time detection and estimation for early safety warnings. The ICVMP has the feature of seamless data transmission, and the uplink and downlink data transmission time is less than 8 ms, which provides a basis for the online implementation of the data-driven model. In our work, the accuracy, robustness, and computing time of the proposed method are verified in the practical application V2X system, an intelligent connected vehicle management platform (ICVMP) system, with the experimental data. This paper focuses on the SoH of the intelligent cloud BMS, which consists of health feature extraction and screening, and the realization of the hybrid neural network model.

The battery aging dataset utilized in this work was obtained by experiments from a battery cycling life test platform (Fan et al., 2021). Six 18650 power cells (Prospower ICR18650P) were selected from the same batch, and the specifications are displayed in Table 1. These cells were aged with the same cycling profile, including the battery pretreatment cycle, aging cycle and capacity calibrating cycle. The specific steps of the cycling profile are provided in Table 2. All the cells were cycled in a humidity chamber (SANWOOD SMG-150-CC) at a constant temperature of 25 °C. The current and voltage characteristics of the cycling test are shown in Fig. 2(a). This dataset, with a specific temperature and cycling profile, is used for health feature extraction, screening and real-time deep learning model research in the first stage. Further work will focus on the extension of the aging cycling profiles and temperature.

It is known that the cycle life of a battery is determined by the essential properties of the battery. The essential attributes of a battery can be expressed through its external characteristics, including voltage, current, internal resistance, and capacity. These parameters can reflect the deterioration and health of the battery. LIBs usually have three states under actual conditions: charge, discharge, and rest. As the operating states of EVs and other equipment continue to change, the current of a battery in the working state will also change accordingly, so it is difficult to extract features for deep learning model research. In the resting state, the internal and external parameters of a battery hardly change or change slowly, and it is difficult to observe the regularity. In comparison, in both the practical application of a device and the development process of a battery, the charging process is usually constant and always controlled. Therefore, external parameters such as battery current and voltage can be easily and accurately measured. In this paper, the terminal voltage and terminal current of a battery are measured for health feature extraction. The typical conditions and parameters of the charging data are shown in Table 3.

The battery properties change as the SoH varies. Accordingly, the internal properties and external performance of batteries with different cycle lives or at different cycle numbers are different, which leads to different terminal voltages, currents, surface temperatures, and curve shapes during charging. Fig. 2(b) shows the curve shapes of the charge voltage of a battery at different cycle numbers. The voltage–curve features, such as the value, endpoint of the curve and duration of charging, are all different in different cycles. In addition, the rising rate and decreasing rate of the charging curve also vary. These characteristics reflect the battery SoH value, which is shown in Fig. 2(c).

Feature extraction is a key step in the SoH estimation of LIBs and not only affects the computational effort of the algorithm but also enables the determination of the accuracy of SoH estimation. For the SoH estimation problem, the common feature inputs can be mainly classified into model-dependent features, direct measurement features, and second-order processing features. Among them, model-dependent features mainly refer to ohmic resistance, polarization resistance, polarization capacity, etc. These features depend on complex models and are not suitable for real-time applications. The direct measurement features, such as the number of cycles, voltage, current, and temperature, are recorded by the sensors in the BMS, while the second-order processing features refer to the second-order features obtained by a simple calculation of the direct measurement features, such as IC/DV curves (Berecibar et al., 2016) and dV/dt curves (Wu et al., 2016).

Actually, it is easy to obtain the observed voltage, current and temperature quantities for any battery. Fig. 2(b) illustrates the charging voltage variation with time for different cycles, where the battery life decay is represented by the gradient color from dark blue to light blue. The battery voltage curve during charging is relatively stable due to the fixed CC charging method, and there is a regular change with the aging of LIBs. Therefore, a very clear pattern exists between the voltage and SoH, and this voltage segment can be chosen as a direct input for estimating SoH. As the battery cycle proceeds, the battery capacity will gradually decay, and the battery charging voltage curve will also change. The trend of the battery charging voltage curve remains the same as the battery capacity decays, but the time required to increase the same voltage magnitude gradually decreases. According to this characteristic, the method of calculating the cumulative time of the charging voltage is chosen to estimate the SoH value for different cycles. The voltage curves for different cycles of the CC charging mode were selected to analyze the tested battery. Obviously, the charging time for the CC mode becomes shorter as the cycle period increases. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the method of comparing the areas enclosed by the charging voltage curves is used to evaluate the degree of influence of SoH in different cycles. Because the battery charging data collected in the cycling test are discrete data, the linear difference equation Eq. (1) is applied to more accurately calculate the enclosed area of the charging voltage curve in different cycles.
(1)

∑
n = 1

denotes the linear function of the charging curve,

denotes the linear function of the time series.

represent the time of the current moment and last the moment.

Based on the battery life characteristics discussed in Section 2.1, the features of the current and voltage curves are extracted and divided into two groups. Both groups are related to capacity, charge time, and voltage drop at the CV stage.

Group 1:
T

indicates the initial absolute time when the charging value reaches 3.4 V (in practical application, it indicates the absolute time of charging initiation). Furthermore,
T

are the total charging times for each 50 mV rise in the charging voltage from 3.40 V to 4.20 V.

T

.

To select the features that are highly correlated with the capacity decay of LIBs and improve the accuracy and computational efficiency of LIB capacity estimation, this paper adopts feature correlation analysis based on the Pearson correlation coefficient and selects the features as the input feature sequences of the final battery capacity estimation model by the analysis results. The Pearson correlation coefficient is a parameter that measures the degree of correlation between factors based on the similarity or dissimilarity of the trends between them (Benesty et al., 2009). The output ranges from −1 to +1, with 0 representing no correlation, negative values representing a negative correlation, and positive values representing a positive correlation. There are two variables, X and Y. Then, the Pearson correlation coefficient between the two variables can be calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
ρ
X , Y
=
c o v
( X , Y )
σ
X
σ
Y
=
E
[
( X −
μ
X
)

μ
X
)
]
σ
X
σ
Y

The above equation defines the overall correlation coefficient, which is generally expressed as
ρ
X , Y
. The estimation of the covariance and variance based on the sample yields the sample standard deviation, which is generally expressed as r:
(3)
r =
∑
i = 1
n

)

)

)
2

Y

¯
)
2

The Pearson correlation coefficients of 31 feature sequences extracted from the charging voltage curve are calculated separately, and the common line features are found according to the correlation coefficients between the features. The collinearity property refers to the existence of a strong or even complete linear correlation between the independent variables. This leads to a decrease in the predictive capability of the model and increases the cost of the interpretation of the model results. The analysis results are shown in Fig. 3(b). As seen in the figure, the correlation of the time interval (T0-T15) is very high and belongs to the common line features, and areas of the time intervals A1 and A2 have the same correlations. Therefore, one of the time interval features and all of the area features are retained (T1 is retained). Only one of the A1 and A2 features is retained because of the same correlations (A2 is retained). Thus, the features that are input to the network are (n represents the length of features):
I n p u t =
[ T 1 , A 2 , A 3 , A 3 , … , A 15 ]
n × 15

To demonstrate the effectiveness of using Pearson correlation coefficients to exclude colinear features, this paper again adopts the lightGBM method to fit the importance of all features relative to SoH. The essence of lightGBM is a histogram algorithm, which takes up less memory and has a lower data separation complexity. The idea is to discretize continuous floating-point features into k discrete values and construct a histogram of width k. Then, the training data are traversed, and the cumulative statistics of each discrete value in the histogram are counted. For feature selection, it is only necessary to iterate through the histogram to find the optimal segmentation point based on the discrete values of the histogram.

Fig. 3(c) shows the results of the feature sequence importance ranking, and the top 15 features with the highest importance are selected as the feature signals of each battery. Finally, the selected features are introduced into the network model proposed in this paper for training to effectively predict the capacity of lithium-ion batteries.

Different from a previous study that predicted SoH at future time steps based on historical steps (Tan and Zhao, 2020), this paper uses transfer learning to obtain a network estimation model based on source battery data and then transfers the network model to the estimation module to estimate the SoH of the tested battery, as shown in Fig. 4.

In addition, to fully utilize the source battery data to obtain the best estimation model, for each cycle, the charging voltage profile is segmented into multiple data segments of length
Δ Q
, where the interval between each initial voltage is 50 mV. Then, all samples in the source dataset are randomly shuffled, and the data segments are drawn at fixed intervals as validation data. For the tested battery, the segmented charging curves of all cycles are imported into the DSMTNet model to estimate the battery capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed hybrid neural network model for SoH estimation
ce:section.ce:para: Developing estimation networks is the core task to achieve battery SoH estimation. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the developed DSMTNet model contains three types of operations, i.e., multilayer perceptron (MLP), 1DCNN, and self-attention mechanism operations. In these operations, the input sequence is embedded into high dimensions using the MLP, the features of the sequence data are extracted and vectorized using the 1DCNN, and the attention mechanism clusters the information between the feature vectors to retain as many individual and common features as possible. Finally, the dependencies between the high-level features and the corresponding battery capacity values are obtained by reducing the dimension through the MLP and the fully connected layer.

The architecture of the proposed method for time series forecasting is shown in Fig. 5(a). A three-head structure is chosen to encode the sequence for better expression. A general and illustrative overview of the approach is as follows.

First, the feature encoder learns to observe the representation of the battery sequence data, mainly through the battery sequence encoder. For each sample of battery sequence data, a 1DCNN is applied to extract features from the sequence data across the time steps and encode them into a vector, resulting in encoding
V
s
( i )
for battery i.
(4)
V
s
( i )
= C N N
( M L
P
e m b
(
X
i
,
W
e m b
)
,
W
C N N
)
where
X
i
denotes the input data of the networks,
W
e m b
denotes the weight of the MLP layer, and
W
C N N
denotes the weight of the convolutional layer.

Then, these vectors are sent to the attention layer for further representation learning. More specifically, the attention layer is designed to tackle feature extraction and learning from the input data. The input data
D =
(
x
1
,
y
1
)
,
(
x
2
,
y
2
)
, … ,
(
x
n
,
y
n
)
are a dataset containing a collection of pairs
(
x
i
,
y
i
)
, where
x
i
is the time series consisting of i ordered values with
y
i
as its output values.
Finally, the feature vector with reduced dimensionality by the MLP is processed by the fully connected layer, and the estimation module produces the output values of the network.

In previous research on battery SoH estimation based on deep learning, the RNN approach has been widely proposed to learn and capture temporal features (Chen et al., 2021). However, due to their inherent sequential nature, RNNs have difficulty capturing and computing long sequences, which results in poor accuracy and consumes much time when performing long time sequence estimations (Pascanu et al., 2013). Since the convolutional structure has proven its ability to capture features and perform parallel computations, it can be combined with multiple filters. A standard convolutional structure can be considered a single-headed architecture. A multiheaded convolutional structure (Xiao et al., 2021b) simply multiplies this representational learning capability. For multiple heads, multiheaded CNNs can have different filter sets and different processing layers in each head. For example, in this research, a three-headed CNN is proposed, and in the first layer, 9, 7 and 5 filters per head are used. By using multiple heads, CNNs have the unique ability to combine various feature learning to process an input sequence, thus enriching the extracted features and improving the final representation learning results.

However, through observing the training process of convolution network parameters, the optimization is always overfitted to mid fields, which are strongly relevant to similarity on battery data. It leads to the result that dissimilar parts usually underfit. To solve this problem, an attention unit is proposed to learn the differential distribution of features and activate different parts of neurons of various fields. It ensures that similar and dissimilar fields both cause effects during training. The architecture of the attention unit is shown in Fig. 5(b). Global and local attention units are chosen for DSMTNet, in which global and local differ in terms of whether the “attention” is placed on all source positions or on only a few source positions, to make the method more robust and effective.

Local Attention: To aggregate the similarity between each battery node, average pooling is usually used to pool the data of multiple batteries into a single line; however, it is known that taking the average will lead to the results tending to the center of the group (both universal characteristics of the battery), and missing outliers in the data will lead to individualized characteristics being discarded. Therefore, each battery data point is treated as a node, and the local attention computational mechanism is adopted to activate the local characteristics of each battery node, as follows:
(5)

denotes the transformation matrix,
E

denotes the all battery node,
E

denotes the target battery node, and
E
l o c a
l
s
is the local information of the population battery node,
a ∈
R
h
represents weights of fields. u refers to a content-based function that inputs encoder output into the full connected layer, then the dot product with all nodes.

Global attention: In addition to proprietary information, there is also shared information related to the composition that is common among batteries. Therefore, a global attention mechanism is adopted to capture this part of the group information.
(8)
v =
E
s
T
t a n h
(
W

s
)
(9)

=

T

g l o b a

= b
E
s
where
W
g
∈
R
s × h
denotes a fully connected matrix,
E
s
denotes the all battery node and
E
g l o b a
l
s
is the global information of the battery node. Essentially, this is self-attention, where features that are more similar to the population distribution will be extracted.
b ∈
R
h
represents weights of fields. v refers to a content-based function that concatenates all encoder output vectors to one dimension, then the dot product with all nodes.

Finally, the output of the attention mechanism calculates the global similarity and the local similarity, which is defined as:
(11)
S
u , s
= α ⋅ c o s
(
E
u
,
E
g l o b a
l
s
)
+ β ⋅ c o s
(
E
u
,
E
l o c a
l
s
)

To evaluate the performance, some evaluation criteria are needed to assess the accuracy of the estimation model, and in this paper, the mean absolute error (MAE), maximum error (MAXE), root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) are utilized for evaluation (Emmert-Streib and Dehmer, 2019).
(12)
M A E =
1
n
∑
t = 1
n
| f
x
t
−
y
t
|
(13)
M A X E = m a x
| f
x
t
−
y
t
|
(14)
R M S E =
∑
t = 1
n
f
x
t
−
y
t
2
n
(15)
M A P E =
100 %
n
∑
i = 1
n
|
f
x
t
−
y
i
y
i
|
where
y
t
is the actual value at moment t,
f
x
t
is the predicted value at moment t, and n is the number of observations.

The features extracted from each lithium-ion battery charging curve are utilized as the input of DSMTNet, and the value of SoH is the output. To optimize the initial weights of the network, the mean square error (MSE) (Köksoy, 2006) is chosen as the fitness function, the initial step size and the number of iterations are set to 1 and 200, respectively, and the learning rate is 5e-5. The network adopts the rectified linear unit (ReLU) function as the activation function in the training phase. When the network is initialized, all weights are initialized with the values from truncated normal distribution sampling with the mean 0 and the standard deviation 0.01. All deviations are initialized to 0. Such an initialization can prevent the neural network from falling into a local optimum to some extent. Then, the loss function of the neural network is optimized using the gradient descent method with a sufficient Adam learning rate (Kingma and Ba, 2017). An early stopping mechanism is established to prevent the model from overfitting. Specifically, the training process is terminated if the monitored validation loss does not decrease for k time points.

To determine the efficacy of the feature selection methods, three comparison experiments are conducted to forecast the SoH values of the three batteries with the removed features, all features, and the selected features. The estimation curves of all features and the selected features are depicted in Fig. 6(a)–(c), demonstrating that both input techniques produce more accurate estimations, demonstrating the efficiency of the feature extraction method. In addition, Table 4 displays the error criteria of estimation results for three conditions. It is evident from the table that the removed features are invalid, as the RMSE and MAXE of the estimation results for the removed features are significantly higher than those of the methods that use all features and those that use no features.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the estimation results of the different models
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the effectiveness of the method, this paper uses six common methods for comparison, including random forest (RF), decision tree (DT), adaptive boosting (Adaboost), feedforward neural network (FNN), three-headed BiLSTM and 1DCNN. The schematics of the three deep learning models are shown in Table 5. The SoH estimation results for B04, B05 and B06 are shown in Fig. 7. From the figures, the estimated results of DSMTNet are closer to the real battery SoH curve. The evaluation criteria of estimating the SoH values obtained by different methods are given in Table 6, which shows that the DSMTNet model proposed in this paper estimates the three battery SoH values with MAEs of 0.0063, 0.0053, and 1.05% and RMSEs of 0.0089, 0.0068, and 1.31%, which means that the errors obtained by the proposed algorithm while estimating the lithium-ion battery capacities are significantly reduced.

In addition, the number of parameters and the estimation operation times are explored for the four methods. Three groups of battery data with the same sequence lengths were taken for estimation, and each had dimensions of [1000,15]. To make the test more convincing, each model was estimated three times to acquire average times. Table 7 compares the numbers of parameters and the running times of the models, and it can be seen that the proposed DSMTNet model has a better performance.

In this study, the charging curves are segmented into three datasets from three different locations and input into the network to investigate the effect of different segmentation locations on the battery SoH estimation. Table 8 lists the initial and ending points of the charging voltage profiles for segments A, B and C, where L represents the total length of the individual cell charging voltage sequence. An RMSE and MAXE comparison of the estimation results of the seven methods with segment A, B and C data as input is shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b). In the figures, it can be seen that the RMSEs and MAXE of DSMTNet have the best performance for B04 and B05 batteries. In particular, the RMSEs of all models on the segment C data exceed 0.02, but the proposed DSMTNet method also has the best estimation performance.

To further investigate the effect of the lengths of different positions of data on the SoH estimation results, the data are partitioned into five subsets by fixed coefficients from 0.3 to 0.5, as shown in Table 9. Fig. 8(c) and (d) shows the RMSEs and MAXE of the seven methods for the capacity estimation of different lengths. For all methods, the RMSEs and MAXE of SoH estimation increase with the increase in the segmentation factors. The RMSE and MAXE of the proposed DSMTNet method are the smallest for all length cases.

In this paper, DSMTNet combined with a novel feature analysis and screening method is proposed to solve the accuracy, robustness, and real-time problems of data-based SoH estimation. The main contributions are as follows:

The correlation between health features and SoH was evaluated by Pearson’s correlation coefficient approach. Then, the most relevant features were screened out by the lightGBM method and input into DSMTNet. These feature disposal methods can significantly improve the accuracy of SoH estimation, in which the MaxE and RMSE of SoH estimation decrease by 53.85% and 28.42%, respectively.

The proposed DSMTNet model utilizes multiple head neural networks for multiscale feature extraction of voltage, current, and SoH data sequences of batteries to learn the temporal relationships of the sequential data. A dual attention mechanism is used to capture the local and global information of batteries to explore the common and differentiated information in the battery sequence data. Compared with RF, DT, Adaboost, FNN, BiLSTM, and 1DCNN, the MAE, RMSE, MaxE, and MAPE of DSMTNet are decreased by 72.25%, 69.51%, 61.13%, and 72.04%, respectively. The computing time is reduced by 89.67%; hence, the real-time characteristics of the model can be guaranteed. Even if 1/3 of the data segment is available, the RMSE can still be maintained at less than 0.02. In general, the proposed DSMTNet model is suitable for application in EVs with a cooperative vehicle infrastructure system.

Referring to the application situation of EV charging in a relatively stable environment (containing current and voltage data), we use experimental charging data and segmented data (partial charging curve) to evaluate the SoH model based on the ICVMP. In the next stage, the proposed method will be validated by actual vehicle data in the ICVMP system with the support of other team members. In addition to the deep learning method, it is important to note that battery health feature analysis and screening require additional study to increase the accuracy and real-time performance of deep learning.

Huanyu Wang: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Jun Li: Investigation, Visualization. Xiaoxi Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Jun Rao: Investigation, Visualization. Yuqian Fan: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review and editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaojun Tan: Investigation, Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province, China(2019B090910002, 2020B0909040004).
